result.markdown:
# ProPlotFits — An Open Letter to Salvy

*Querido Salvador. Un nativo de KC te habla.*

---

## Dear Salvy,

You probably get a lot of letters. Most of them are from people who saw you throw out a baserunner from your knees, or hit a walk-off in the ninth, or laugh that big laugh in the dugout that makes the whole stadium feel like a backyard cookout. Those letters are deserved.

This one is a little different.

I'm a data scientist from Kansas City. I grew up here, in the middle of the country, in a place that most people assume is — let's say — culturally straightforward. One language. One kind of fan. One kind of story.

They're wrong. And you, more than almost anyone, are proof of that.

You came from Valencia, Venezuela. You learned this city's rhythms while this city slowly learned yours. You became the most beloved Royal of your generation not despite where you came from, but because of who you are — and who you are carries both worlds at once. Bilingual. Binational. Completely, authentically Kansas City.

I'm starting something called **ProPlotFits**. It's a sports analytics podcast and data blog, and it lives at the intersection of the two things I love most: numbers and language. Every episode will have a real chart, a real model, a real statistical argument about this team we both love. And it will move between English and Spanish the way a real conversation between two KC fans actually does — not translated, not subtitled, just spoken.

The Latino community in this city is bigger than people think. The map below is proof of that — Spanish speakers, by census tract, across the Kansas City metro. Jackson County. Clay. Platte. Cass. Johnson County in Kansas. Wyandotte. Leavenworth. Royal Blue, because what other color would we use.

This show is for them. It's for the kid in Wyandotte County who loves baseball and knows the stats but has never heard anyone break down a WAR calculation in the language his abuela speaks. It's for the fan in Johnson County who grew up watching you and never saw himself reflected in sports media.

It's also, honestly, a love letter to this city and this team — told through data, told bilingually, told with the same pride you wear on your chest every time you strap on that gear at Kauffman.

So: *gracias, Salvy*. For showing up. For staying. For being exactly who you are in exactly this place.

We're just trying to do the same thing with a microphone and a dataset.

— *ProPlotFits, Kansas City*

---

## El mapa / The Map

### Spanish speakers across the KC metro, by census tract

The following R code pulls American Community Survey data for the seven-county KC metro and renders a choropleth of Hispanic households by tract — shaded in Royal Blue.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
```

```{.r .cell-code}
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# Pull state boundaries for MO and KS
state_borders <- states(cb = TRUE, year = 2020) %>%
  filter(STUSPS %in% c("MO", "KS")) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)


# -------------------------------------------------------------
# Census variable: B16001_003E
# "Speak Spanish" — from table B16001 (Language Spoken at Home)
# ACS 5-year estimates
# -------------------------------------------------------------

# Set your Census API key if not already done:
census_api_key("187f5aac4952d3a712229e1f84b52a31d52ee628", install = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}
```
Your API key has been stored in your .Renviron and can be accessed by Sys.getenv("CENSUS_API_KEY"). 
To use now, restart R or run `readRenviron("~/.Renviron")`
```
:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
[1] "187f5aac4952d3a712229e1f84b52a31d52ee628"
```
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
kc_counties <- list(
  MO = c("Jackson", "Clay", "Platte", "Cass"),
  KS = c("Johnson", "Wyandotte", "Leavenworth")
)

pull_hispanic_pop <- function(state, counties) {
  get_decennial(
    geography = "tract",
    variables = c(hispanic_pop = "P2_002N"),
    state     = state,
    county    = counties,
    year      = 2020,
    sumfile   = "dhc",
    geometry  = TRUE
  )
}

kc_mo <- pull_hispanic_pop("MO", kc_counties$MO)
```

```{.r .cell-code}
# Dissolve county tracts to state-level outlines (only your 7 counties)
mo_outline <- kc_mo %>% st_union()
ks_outline <- kc_ks %>% st_union()

kc_metro <- bind_rows(kc_mo, kc_ks) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)

# -------------------------------------------------------------
# Royal Blue palette
# Official KC Royals Royal Blue: #174B8B
# Build a sequential ramp from near-white to full Royal Blue
# -------------------------------------------------------------

royal_blue_dark  <- "#174B8B"
royal_blue_light <- "#D6E4F5"

ggplot(kc_metro) +
  geom_sf(
    aes(fill = value),
    color     = "white",
    linewidth = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_sf(
    data      = mo_outline,
    fill      = NA,
    color     = royal_blue_dark,
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  geom_sf(
    data      = ks_outline,
    fill      = NA,
    color     = royal_blue_dark,
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low      = royal_blue_light,
    high     = royal_blue_dark,
    na.value = "#EEEEEE",
    name     = "Hispanic Population",
    labels   = scales::comma
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Population of Hispanic Descent across the KC metro by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "",
    caption  = "Source: 2020 Census · Table P2_002N · ProPlotFits"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_family = "sans") +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold",
                                   color = royal_blue_dark, margin = margin(b = 6)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "#555555", margin = margin(b = 12)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "#888888", margin = margin(t = 10)),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  color = "#333333"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8,  color = "#555555"),
    plot.margin     = margin(16, 16, 16, 16)
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](QueridoSalvador_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



### What to look for

Wyandotte County (Kansas City, KS) will light up darkest — it has one of the highest concentrations of Spanish-speaking households of any county in Kansas. A significant cluster also runs through the urban core of Jackson County on the Missouri side. The Johnson County suburbs tell a different story: the numbers are lower per tract but the footprint is spreading.

That spread is the story. The KC Latino community is not confined to one zip code or one side of the state line. It is distributed, growing, and present — across school districts, across county lines, across both Kansas and Missouri.

It is, in other words, exactly as complicated and interesting as the city itself.

---

## Coming up on ProPlotFits

- **Ep 2 — Salvy by the numbers**: A full statistical portrait. Catcher WAR, framing metrics, caught-stealing percentage, durability. Why the numbers confirm what the eyes already knew.
- **Ep 3 — La máquina del pitcheo**: Statcast spin rate and movement profiles on Royals pitchers, explained in both languages.
- **Ep 4 — The pipeline mapa**: Where do Royals prospects come from? A farm system origin story told through geography.

*Nos vemos en el diamante.*
